summary: AH8 'Complete Threat/Patch Linkage' table omits P-016, P-021, P-024, P-025, and all of P-029–P-049 despite these being ACTIVE with named annex references in Patch_Log.
why_it_matters: The master linkage table is the authoritative cross-reference; its incompleteness means auditors and readers cannot rely on AH8 for complete chain verification.
recommended_action: Add all missing ACTIVE patches to AH8 and retitle it to reflect its actual partial-sessions scope, or document the intentional omission policy.

summary: P-013 heading appears twice in Patch_Log.md, causing anchor slug collision (#p-013 vs #p-013-2).
why_it_matters: Any internal link pointing to #p-013 resolves to the first (incorrect) occurrence; the reader's deduplication makes the correct heading effectively inaccessible.
recommended_action: Remove or merge the duplicate P-013 heading and verify all anchor links to it resolve correctly.

summary: ANNEX_AU is absent from the double-letter sequence (AT→AV) with no documentation in INDEX.md audit history.
why_it_matters: Undocumented gaps in the annex sequence confuse contributors and readers and may indicate a merged or accidentally omitted annex.
recommended_action: Add an audit-history entry to INDEX.md stating whether AU was never assigned, merged, or is reserved.

summary: P-007, P-010, and P-028 are absent from the patch sequence (P-001 through P-062) with no documented retirement, merge, or skip explanation.
why_it_matters: Undocumented gaps in a sequential register make the numbering untrustworthy and prevent auditors from knowing whether a patch was silently dropped.
recommended_action: Add a retirement/skip note for each missing patch ID, as done for T-003 in the Threat Register.

summary: T-003 is absent as a standalone entry with no audit history note; T-010/T-011 are merged as a single heading without an explanation note in the Threat Register itself.
why_it_matters: Cross-documents (ANNEX_AT, Annual_Compound_Simulation) still use T-003 with different meanings, indicating the retirement was not communicated corpus-wide.
recommended_action: Add explicit T-003 retirement notes to ANNEX_AT and Annual_Compound_Simulation and add an audit entry to the Threat Register explaining its absorption.

summary: Hardening_Queue.md uses DESIGNED/ACTIVE-UNPROVEN/ONGOING shorthand rather than canonical vocabulary; machine parsing via status_for() reads non-canonical labels.
why_it_matters: Automated corpus tools that parse status fields will misread or drop non-canonical values, undermining the status discipline requirement.
recommended_action: Replace shorthand status labels with the exact canonical vocabulary defined in CLAUDE.md.

summary: docs/PLAIN_LANGUAGE.md exists on disk but is not in CORE_DOCS and is not reached by any glob, making it silently absent from the reader corpus.
why_it_matters: The file contains plain-language explanations intended for public readers but is invisible through the reader app.
recommended_action: Register in CORE_DOCS in export_corpus.py or add a note in the file explaining its intentional exclusion.

summary: Full review document is excluded from the corpus and unlinked from any corpus doc, making it invisible to readers despite containing substantive constitutional analysis.
why_it_matters: Review findings are invisible to auditors consulting the reader app, reducing the value of recorded review work.
recommended_action: Link from Provenance_Map.md or Architecture_Source_Map.md, or register in the corpus.

summary: Single-letter annex slots E and F are absent with no mention in INDEX.md audit history — unclear if ever assigned or deliberately skipped.
why_it_matters: If E and F were assigned and later removed without documentation, content may be missing from the corpus.
recommended_action: UNCERTAIN — add audit history entries for E and F, even if only 'never assigned.'

summary: P-056 row in AH8 appears to have shifted columns — Patch ID appears in the Threat column, title in the Patch column, Annex in the Status column.
why_it_matters: Column transposition in the master linkage table makes the row untrustworthy for machine-readable parsing and misleads manual readers.
recommended_action: Correct the P-056 row (and verify all subsequent PROPOSED rows) to match the table header column order.

summary: P-056 and ANNEX_AK §AK8 both reference ANNEX_AZ §AZ2 as the Tier 0 token mechanism spec, but §AZ2 contains demurrage definitions (S, M*, W*), not a token mechanism — the content is marked '(to be drafted).'
why_it_matters: INV-LAUNCH-1 is gated on this spec; the gate document is missing or misaddressed, making the pre-operational prerequisite unverifiable.
recommended_action: Create ANNEX_AZ §AZ2 with the token mechanism specification, or update all references to the correct location.

summary: P-007, P-010, and P-028 are absent from the patch sequence with no retirement notice, merger note, or explanation.
why_it_matters: Three undocumented gaps in a sequence claimed to run P-001 through P-062 undermine the audit trail for anyone verifying completeness.
recommended_action: Add explicit retirement or merge notes for P-007, P-010, and P-028 in the Patch Log.

summary: At least three Threat Register detailed entries carry 'Status: PROPOSED' for patches (P-034, P-014, P-015, P-024) that are ACTIVE per the Patch Log and AH8.
why_it_matters: A reader trusting the Threat Register's detailed entries would believe Critical/High mitigations are not yet active, undermining the register's governance function.
recommended_action: Update all detailed threat entries to reflect current patch status from Patch_Log.md.

summary: AH8 PROPOSED entries (P-056+) have columns in a different order than the table header, breaking consistent machine-readable parsing of the master linkage table.
why_it_matters: Automated tools and future auditors parsing AH8 will misattribute all fields for recent PROPOSED patches.
recommended_action: Reformat all PROPOSED entry rows in AH8 to match the table header column order (Threat | Patch | Status | Annex | Session).

section: Summary by Priority table and Retired/Reserved table
summary: T-010 appears in both the Summary by Priority table (as an active entry) and the Retired/Reserved table (as RENUMBERED), creating dual presence with conflicting treatment.
why_it_matters: Readers may follow T-010 references expecting active content and find retired content, or vice versa, corrupting traceability for the narrative threat cluster.
recommended_action: Remove T-010 from the Summary by Priority table and ensure all narrative threat references redirect to T-011.

summary: T-016 detailed entry annotates P-034 as 'PROPOSED' but P-034 is ACTIVE in the Patch Log inventory, AH8, and its own detailed entry.
why_it_matters: Readers assessing T-016 mitigation status will believe the two-key architectural precondition is unactivated when it is active.
recommended_action: Update T-016 detailed entry to reflect P-034 ACTIVE status.

summary: P-024 lists 'TR-07' in the Related Threat column — an ID that does not exist in the Threat Register and has no discoverable registry.
why_it_matters: An unregistered namespace ID in the master inventory breaks back-traceability for a Critical-priority ACTIVE patch.
recommended_action: Resolve TR-07 to its canonical T-ID or document the TR namespace in a registry note.

section: P-030, P-031, P-032, P-033 inventory rows
summary: PRD-004, PRD-008, PRD-009 IDs appear as Related Threat values for four ACTIVE patches but PRD- has no discoverable registry in the corpus.
why_it_matters: Four ACTIVE patches (including the anti-dynasty ownership and stewardship rules) have unverifiable threat sources, making their constitutional justification untraceable.
recommended_action: Define the PRD namespace in a registry note or cross-reference it to the canonical T-ID equivalent.

section: P-057 through P-062 entries; ANNEX_AH.md AH8
summary: ACL- IDs (ACL-005, ACL-007, ACL-009, ACL-010, ACL-011, ACL-012) are referenced as threats for six Critical PROPOSED patches forming the pilot-town founding framework, but no ACL registry exists in the corpus.
why_it_matters: Six Critical-priority PROPOSED patches cannot be back-traced to formal threat definitions, making the pilot-town founding framework untraceable.
recommended_action: Create an ACL (Abuse Case Library) cross-reference or register ACL IDs in a findable location.

summary: IC-004 is referenced in P-045's Related Threat but no IC- register exists — IC-004 is addressed by P-045/AH5.1 but cannot be back-traced to a formal threat definition.
why_it_matters: The Dignity-Only Continuity Mode (AH5.1) is a Critical fail-safe mechanism whose threat basis is unregistered.
recommended_action: Register IC-004 in the Threat Register or document the IC namespace as an integrity-check sub-system with a pointer to its definition.

summary: T-022 (Electoral Cycle Capture) has its primary structural defense (P-018) PROPOSED, meaning the most complete hostile-government-capture failure mode lacks an ACTIVE primary control.
why_it_matters: The corpus design acknowledges T-022 as Critical severity; its PROPOSED-only primary patch means the threat is at Designed status, not Active-unproven.
recommended_action: Prioritize P-018 activation or formally document the CIP (P-051) as a sufficient interim control pending P-018.

summary: The Acceptance Protocol Priority table is patch-centric with no column linking back to specific constitutional clause IDs or invariant IDs.
why_it_matters: A Phase 2 adversarial audit cannot map acceptance tests back to the specific constitutional text being protected.
recommended_action: Add a clause/invariant column to the Priority Acceptance Order table.

summary: Household demurrage safe harbor is stated as 6 months in the Constitution but 18 months in ANNEX_J §J2 — a 3× quantitative contradiction in a financial implementation parameter.
why_it_matters: An implementation team reading the Constitution builds the wrong system; the Constitution controls, so ANNEX_J's reasoned 18-month derivation is functionally overridden by an underivable 6-month figure.
recommended_action: Align the Constitution and ANNEX_J safe harbor to the same value and include the derivation rationale in the Constitution.

summary: T-022 Complete Register Summary lists only P-018 and omits ACTIVE P-051 (the Constitutional Integrity Panel), which is a materially different and currently active control.
why_it_matters: Decision-makers consulting the Complete Summary for T-022 mitigation status will not know the CIP exists or is active.
recommended_action: Add P-051 to the T-022 entry in the Complete Register Summary.

summary: ANNEX_AM header shows 'Status: PROPOSED' (reflecting P-018) while §AM8 implements ACTIVE P-051 — a mixed status not disclosed in the header.
why_it_matters: Readers may skip ANNEX_AM as a PROPOSED document and miss the ACTIVE CIP provisions in §AM8.
recommended_action: Update ANNEX_AM header to reflect mixed status (e.g., 'Partly ACTIVE via P-051 §AM8; P-018 sections PROPOSED') with explicit section mapping.

summary: T-022 detailed entry records 'Status: Designed (P-018 / Annex AM PROPOSED)' and does not acknowledge ACTIVE P-051.
why_it_matters: The detailed entry perpetuates the view that T-022 has no active defense, directly contradicting P-051 ACTIVE status.
recommended_action: Update the T-022 detailed entry to acknowledge P-051 and revise the status to 'Active — unproven.'

summary: 'Partial' is used as a section heading in the Threat Register — a forbidden status vocabulary term per corpus contracts.
why_it_matters: Forbidden vocabulary leakage in a primary governance document undermines the status discipline requirement.
recommended_action: Replace 'Partial' heading with approved vocabulary (e.g., 'Active — unproven with remaining work').

summary: 'ADDRESSED*' is used as a status note for T-020 and T-021 — 'Addressed' is a forbidden status term per corpus contracts.
why_it_matters: Forbidden vocabulary in the resolution matrix signals incomplete vocabulary enforcement across governance documents.
recommended_action: Replace 'ADDRESSED*' with the canonical status vocabulary (e.g., 'Active — unproven').

summary: P-018's 70% founding-basket self-executing floor may not guarantee 100% of CSM-category minimums if the founding basket includes non-CSM items, and 'founding basket' is undefined.
why_it_matters: A bad-faith administrator could deliver 70% of the aggregate basket while providing below-CSM quantities of any given essential category, defeating the survival floor intent.
recommended_action: Define 'founding basket' explicitly and add per-category floor requirements to the 70% rule.

summary: P-051 (ACTIVE) adds a mandatory CIP concurrent ratification requirement for all Tier 1 amendments (ANNEX_AM §AM8.6), but neither the Constitution nor architecture/amendment_protocol.md mentions this requirement.
why_it_matters: A Tier 1 amendment executed by the book (Constitution + amendment_protocol.md) would be constitutionally void without the CIP step, but no actor reading the Constitution alone would know this.
recommended_action: Add a CIP concurrent ratification note to the Constitution's Tier 1 amendment clause and to architecture/amendment_protocol.md.

summary: The Constitution describes Tier 1 amendment without mentioning P-034's two-key adversarial attestation pre-condition, which is ACTIVE.
why_it_matters: An actor executing a Tier 1 amendment using only the Constitution as guidance would skip the two-key gate — a Critical-priority active safeguard.
recommended_action: Add a Tier 1 amendment pre-condition reference to the Constitution pointing to P-034 / ANNEX_AV.

summary: The Threat Register records T-022 as 'Designed' with explicit reasoning that no ACTIVE patch exists, but P-051 is ACTIVE for T-022 — the register's own status rule is applied incorrectly to its current data.
why_it_matters: The status assignment rule is documented, auditable, and currently violated — demonstrating the register's status is not maintained in sync with the Patch Log.
recommended_action: Apply the Threat Register's own documented status rule: update T-022 to 'Active — unproven' given P-051 ACTIVE.

summary: ANNEX_AM §AM8.4 requires a '6-of-7 vote of the Federated Ombuds Plenum' to remove a CIP member, but the Federated Ombuds Plenum has 5 members (FC-090) — the threshold is mathematically impossible.
why_it_matters: CIP members cannot be removed through the stated procedure as written; a captured CIP member would be irremovable.
recommended_action: Correct the threshold to a valid fraction of the 5-member Plenum (e.g., 4-of-5) or correct the Plenum size if it was intended to be 7.

summary: 'Multi-key renewal' for Shared Storehouse continuation is referenced in the Constitution but the number of keys, their holders, and revocation procedure are undefined.
why_it_matters: The Shared Storehouse continuation mechanism is a Critical safeguard — an undefined 'multi-key' procedure cannot be operationalized or audited.
recommended_action: Define the multi-key renewal procedure in a named annex or SPECIFICATIONS section.

summary: 'Human dignity' is used as a non-negotiable constitutional floor throughout the corpus but is never defined beyond a physical basket reference.
why_it_matters: Non-physical dignity violations (surveillance, stigma in delivery, forced disclosure) are unenforced because the term's scope is undefined; local actors can argue any non-physical treatment does not violate it.
recommended_action: Add a formal definition of 'human dignity' in the Constitution's definitions section or SPECIFICATIONS covering both physical and non-physical dimensions.

summary: 'Tier 1' and 'Tier 2' are used with three distinct meanings: amendment hierarchy, identity access levels, and identity assurance classes — all active simultaneously with no disambiguation key.
why_it_matters: A governance actor can exploit the ambiguity to argue a Tier-2-assurance decision requires a Tier-2-amendment threshold, creating false procedural barriers or enabling capture.
recommended_action: Add a disambiguation note at first use of each Tier term in every document, or rename the identity tier system to avoid collision with amendment tiers.

summary: 'Independent' is used with at least four distinct standards across the Constitution, only one of which (oracle independence in ANNEX_AL) is formally defined.
why_it_matters: A weak affiliation-only independence standard satisfies constitutional text while gutting the intent; oracle-level independence is not implied by general uses of 'independent.'
recommended_action: Define minimum independence criteria for each governance context (review bodies, oversight panels, oracles) separately.

summary: 'Emergency' is used with three different trigger standards (supply scarcity L2/L3, governance deadlock, Interim Authority activation) with no master definition or disambiguation.
why_it_matters: A bad actor argues for their preferred trigger standard to invoke or block emergency powers; emergency powers invoked outside the intended scope cannot be challenged for procedural invalidity.
recommended_action: Define a master 'emergency' term in the Constitution and enumerate which trigger standard applies in each article context.

summary: 'Enforcement' is mentioned with procedural constraints but the core mechanism — who initiates it, by what steps, with what procedural floor — is unspecified in core articles.
why_it_matters: Enforcement without a procedural floor can cause collateral harm to innocent parties (identified as a failure mode in HC §21) and cannot be challenged for procedural invalidity.
recommended_action: Add a minimum enforcement procedure floor to Article I or VIII of the Constitution, cross-referenced to the relevant annex.

summary: 'Accountability' is used in three distinct senses with no consequence ladder for non-performance by named accountability parties.
why_it_matters: Named accountability parties (keyholders, oversight bodies) face no specified consequence for non-performance; accountability is symbolic rather than enforceable.
recommended_action: Add a consequence ladder for accountability failures cross-referenced to the relevant annex enforcement mechanisms.

summary: 'Qualified independent review body' is the only mechanism to replace a self-interested keyholder but 'qualified' has no defined criteria.
why_it_matters: The keyholders being replaced can block replacement by challenging the petitioner's qualification; conversely any body can invoke the mechanism and claim qualification.
recommended_action: Define minimum criteria for 'qualified' in this context: composition floor, independence standard, appointment source, and challenge process.

summary: 'Corrigibility' is named in P-035 as a constitutional property but is not found in Humane_Constitution.md by grep — the concept may be present under a synonym or the patch may not have been applied.
why_it_matters: P-035 is ACTIVE but the named constitutional property cannot be located in the Constitution; it cannot be tested, enforced, or challenged.
recommended_action: Verify P-035 was applied; if so, add the term 'corrigible' or 'corrigibility' explicitly to the Constitution with a definition.

summary: 'Consent' is used in three distinct senses (jurisdictional community consent with 730-day unwind, individual withdrawal which is immediate, and data/operational consent) that are not formally distinguished.
why_it_matters: An overextended reading could apply the 730-day exit window to individual consent withdrawal, making individual consent withdrawal procedurally impossible.
recommended_action: Add a formal disambiguation note distinguishing jurisdictional consent from individual consent at first constitutional use.

summary: 'Public power' is used as a separation invariant boundary ('Flow cannot buy public power') but the boundary of what counts as public power is never formally defined.
why_it_matters: The separation invariant is unenforceable if the boundary is contested; a wealthy actor can argue their purchased influence is not 'public power.'
recommended_action: Define 'public power' with an enumerated list or bright-line test in SPECIFICATIONS or INVARIANTS.

summary: Constitutional promise of appeal paths is not backed by a minimum procedural floor (timeline, forum, standing) in core articles.
why_it_matters: A constitutional violation outside annex categories has no guaranteed appeal timeline; the right to appeal is nominal without a minimum procedural floor.
recommended_action: Add a minimum appeal floor (timeline, standing, available forums) to Article VIII or the core rights article.

summary: 'Democratic oversight' is named as an ongoing legitimating requirement in INV-007 but has no minimum content definition — no quorum, frequency, representation requirement, or output standard.
why_it_matters: Empty compliance: a governing body holds a minimally attended public session and claims democratic oversight satisfied.
recommended_action: Add minimum procedural content to the democratic oversight requirement: quorum, frequency, representative composition, and output standard.

category: Missing crosswalk between two evidence systems
summary: The six-status label system and eight-level Evidence Ladder coexist without an explicit crosswalk defining how evidence levels map to status transitions.
why_it_matters: Two reviewers examining the same claim can reach different status conclusions using different systems; no mechanism exists to reconcile disagreements.
recommended_action: Add an explicit crosswalk table defining which Evidence Ladder levels are required to transition between each status pair.

summary: 'Safety' is used with at least four distinct referents (physical survival, personal safety, health/safety, system safety) in adjacent clauses without disambiguation.
why_it_matters: 'Safety violation' in adjacent clauses can mean different things, creating enforcement disputes about which safety standard applies.
recommended_action: Prefix 'safety' with its specific domain at each use or add a definitions note distinguishing the four referents.

summary: 'Harm' is used as an enforcement trigger throughout the Constitution without definition or measurable threshold.
why_it_matters: Without a threshold or definition, 'harm' is unquantifiable as an enforcement trigger — different actors will apply wildly different standards.
recommended_action: Define 'harm' with minimum criteria or a reference to the Evidence Ladder level required to establish it.

summary: ANNEX_L §L5 lists remedy types (stay, nullification, rollback, continuity order, sanctions) but provides no criteria for selecting among them.
why_it_matters: Remedies are discretionary; the same violation receives different remedies in different forums, undermining consistency and predictability.
recommended_action: Add remedy selection criteria to §L5: a decision tree or priority ordering tied to violation type and severity.

summary: README.md describes annex range as 'A through AV' but ANNEX_AW, AX, AY, AZ exist and are omitted.
why_it_matters: New readers consulting README.md will not know four annexes (covering whistleblower protection, confidential enrollment, delivery sufficiency, and demurrage architecture) exist.
recommended_action: Update README.md annex range to 'A through AZ' and add one-line summaries for AW–AZ.

summary: ANNEX_AT.md uses retired identifier T-003 to mean 'external dependency capture' — T-003 is permanently retired (absorbed into T-002) per the Threat Register.
why_it_matters: Reuse of a retired ID creates reader confusion and corrupts traceability; future auditors citing T-003 from ANNEX_AT will reach the wrong threat record.
recommended_action: Replace T-003 references in ANNEX_AT with the canonical threat ID(s) that cover external dependency capture.

summary: Annual_Compound_Simulation.md uses T-003 to mean 'Shared Storehouse Scope Creep' — a third distinct meaning for the retired identifier.
why_it_matters: T-003 now means three different things across three documents; any cross-reference to T-003 is ambiguous and corrupts the audit trail.
recommended_action: Replace T-003 in Annual_Compound_Simulation with the current threat ID for Shared Storehouse scope creep.

summary: P-021 (Register Disclosure Protocol) states restricted annex infrastructure is required before it is operative, but no restricted annex file, pointer, or consistency attestation exists in the public corpus.
why_it_matters: P-021's operative status cannot be self-verified from the public corpus; the Threat Register's public/restricted bifurcation is documented but the restricted version's existence cannot be confirmed.
recommended_action: Add a consistency attestation pointer in the public corpus (e.g., a hash, a version note, or a contact record) confirming the restricted annex exists.

summary: Section anchor links using '§ R1–R2', '§ X8', '§ 4.12', '§ AT6.6' notation are not verified by validate_corpus.py; heading renames would produce silent link degradation.
why_it_matters: The corpus validator provides false assurance of reference integrity — all § references could be silently broken and would pass validation.
recommended_action: Extend validate_corpus.py to verify heading-anchor resolution for § notation, or audit all § references manually.

section: T-003 references across ANNEX_AT, Annual_Compound_Simulation, Threat_Register
summary: Retired identifier T-003 has accumulated three distinct meanings across three documents, corrupting the identifier's traceability function.
why_it_matters: Any future audit or remediation action citing T-003 will be ambiguous; cross-document search for T-003 will return contradictory results.
recommended_action: Purge all non-canonical T-003 uses from ANNEX_AT and Annual_Compound_Simulation and replace with correct current threat IDs.

summary: The corpus validator checks file existence for markdown links but does not verify heading anchors (§AH1, §R1–R2, §AT6.6, etc.); silently-missing section headings would not be caught.
why_it_matters: The validator passes cleanly while all § anchor references remain unverified, providing false assurance of reference integrity across the entire corpus.
recommended_action: Add heading-anchor verification to validate_corpus.py for all § references in Provenance_Map.md, Patch_Log.md, Threat_Register.md, and INDEX.md.
